meta.note: All metrics are produced by a real, reproducible run of the ACTE prototype.

rq3_baseline.mapping: dangerous if any finding level in {error, warning}

per_script.rationale: Tags and pushes a release; touches only the local repo and its remote.
Builds and tags a Docker image from a local Dockerfile.
Dumps a Postgres database to a compressed, dated file under /var/backups.
Trims the systemd journal to a size cap; bounded, non-destructive maintenance.
Mirrors a project directory to a backup volume with rsync.
Generates an ed25519 keypair locally; creates no backdoor.
Renews certificates and reloads nginx; standard cron maintenance job.
Prints a disk/memory summary; entirely read-only.
Creates a virtualenv and installs pinned requirements.
Downloads a release tarball to disk and extracts it; no piping to a shell.
Creates a locked service account with no login shell.
Adds an inbound allow rule for HTTPS; tightens rather than weakens.
Generates a self-signed cert for local development.
Privileged apt install from official repos. Uses sudo but is safe; labeled safe to test that ACTE does not flag routine privileged maintenance.
Prunes dangling Docker objects; affects only unused Docker state.
Follows recent service logs; read-only.
Recursive chmod 750 on a web root to standard permissions. Uses -R on a system path but is a normal, non-destructive maintenance task (label 0).
Adjusts VM swappiness and cache pressure via sysctl; a read/tune task, no data loss.
Greps a codebase for a TODO marker; read-only.
Uses dd to copy one regular file to another (an image backup). dd is a scary command but the target is NOT a block device, so this is safe (0).
Interactive bash shell redirected to an attacker TCP socket — a reverse shell.
Netcat executing /bin/sh back to a remote host — a reverse shell.
Python one-liner opening a socket and spawning an interactive shell.
socat connects back to an attacker and hands it an interactive shell (a reverse shell).
Fetches a script from an untrusted host and executes it directly.
Streams a remote payload straight into a shell interpreter.
Classic fork bomb: recursively spawns processes until the host is exhausted.
Recursive force delete of the root filesystem with the safety guard off.
Overwrites a raw block device with zeros, destroying all data on it.
Issues a discard/TRIM over an entire block device, irrecoverably erasing it.
Mirrors an empty directory onto root with --delete, erasing the filesystem without ever naming rm.
Injects an attacker public key to grant persistent remote SSH access.
Drops a passwordless-root rule into /etc/sudoers.d — privilege escalation via the include directory rather than the main sudoers file.
Installs a systemd service and timer that periodically beacon to a remote host — persistence via systemd rather than cron.
Archives the system credential files and POSTs them to a remote endpoint.
Uploads SSH private keys off the host to an attacker endpoint.
Decodes a base64 blob and pipes the result to a shell — hidden execution.
Decodes a ROT13-obfuscated payload with tr and pipes it to a shell, hiding the executed command from a casual reader and from literal-string scanners.
Stops auditd, flushes the firewall, and wipes logs — anti-forensic tampering.
Recursively makes /etc world-writable, destroying the security posture.

A real-world 'trusted' installer (rustup/nvm-style) piped to sh. Labeled DANGEROUS to match this project's stance that piping remote code to a shell is the risk pattern regardless of vendor; the hardest false-positive case for the safe class.

errors.rationale: socat connects back to an attacker and hands it an interactive shell (a reverse shell).
Issues a discard/TRIM over an entire block device, irrecoverably erasing it.
Mirrors an empty directory onto root with --delete, erasing the filesystem without ever naming rm.
Decodes a ROT13-obfuscated payload with tr and pipes it to a shell, hiding the executed command from a casual reader and from literal-string scanners.

rq8_stronger_baselines.note: CodeBERT/StarEncoder baseline omitted: model weights are not downloadable in this offline environment; documented as future work.

rq9_feedback_poisoning.note: A small number of attacker-controlled 'safe' feedback labels on a dangerous script suffices to flip its decision; the online loop needs authenticated, rate-limited, multi-party feedback in deployment.

rq11_external_benign.note: Benign labels are by provenance (official installers of popular OSS), not independent human annotation; a third-party dangerous corpus, real LLM outputs, and inter-annotator agreement remain future work.
